Real Testing Notes: Best for: Creators and bloggers. Simplicity focused on selling products/courses.
column13: Limited templates (minimalist design), basic segmentation compared to others, landing pages are basic, expensive as list grows, no advanced e-commerce features.
column14: TESTED: Used for newsletter with 500 subscribers. Visual automation builder was intuitive (built welcome sequence + product pitch sequence in 1 hour). Tag-based segmentation was simple but effective. However, template selection was limited (minimalist style only). Landing pages were functional but not beautiful. At 1000 subscribers, $29/month is reasonable. Perfect for creators who want simplicity, Mailchimp is better for advanced marketing.
Best for: E-commerce businesses. Most advanced features but can get expensive and complex.
column15: Can get expensive quickly, UI can be overwhelming (too many features), free tier limited to 1 audience, pricing based on subscriber count (not sends), learning curve for advanced features.
column16: TESTED: Used for e-commerce store with 800 subscribers. Segmentation was incredible (could segment by purchase history, engagement, demographics). E-commerce integrations (Shopify, WooCommerce) were seamless. However, pricing jumped to $50+ at 1000 subscribers. UI felt overwhelming with so many features. Perfect for e-commerce needing advanced segmentation, ConvertKit is better for creators.

Real Testing Notes: Best for: Newsletter creators. Optimized for newsletter growth and monetization.
column13: Focused only on newsletters (not general email), limited automation compared to others, segmentation basic, newer platform (fewer integrations), pricing based on subscribers.
column14: TESTED: Started newsletter on Beehiiv. Newsletter-first approach was brilliant (built-in referral program, recommendations, polls). Templates were gorgeous for newsletters. Built landing page and sign-up forms in 30 minutes. However, automation features were basic compared to ConvertKit/Mailchimp. Segmentation was limited. Perfect for newsletter creators who want growth features, use ConvertKit for complex automations.

Limitations: Best for: Writers wanting simple newsletter + paid subscriptions. Dead simple but very limited.
Real Testing Notes: No automation (only broadcasts), no segmentation, limited templates (text only), no landing pages (basic profile only), expensive at scale (10% revenue share), limited features.
column13: TESTED: Started personal newsletter on Substack. Could not be simpler - write, hit send, subscribers get email. Built-in paid subscriptions were brilliant (no setup needed). However, zero automation features (no welcome sequences, no segmentation). No landing page builder (only basic profile). At scale, 10% revenue share is expensive compared to owning your list. Perfect for writers who want dead simple, use ConvertKit for marketing.

Best Use Case: Best for: Design-conscious small businesses. Best pricing at scale (unlimited subscribers).
Limitations: Limited automation features, basic segmentation, fewer integrations, no e-commerce features, limited analytics, newer platform (fewer resources).
Real Testing Notes: TESTED: Used for boutique newsletter. Template designs were stunning (best looking of any platform). Flat-rate pricing ($59/mo unlimited) was incredible at scale (saved $100+ vs Mailchimp at 5000 subs). However, automation features were basic (couldn't do complex branching). Segmentation was limited. Perfect for design-focused businesses with large lists, use Mailchimp for advanced features.
